text.title: Translation Layer and Gate Governance
text.summary: Translation table rules between agile and predictive vocabulary, gate cadence calibration, EVM-on-agile warning, and anti-patterns.

rule.statement: Define one explicit translation table before the first steering report: epic maps to deliverable, story-points map to a declared effort estimate, velocity maps to run-rate. Use the same table for every report cycle and never mix vocabularies within a single audience.
rule.rationale: Vocabulary leakage — "milestone" used in team standups, "increment" in governance packs — causes executives to lose confidence in the program; consistent translation maintains trust.

example.note: Steering-committee pack uses: milestone, deliverable, planned-vs-actual labour, variance. Team ceremony uses: increment, story, velocity, sprint goal. The cadence-translator-agent converts between them; humans never mix the two.

antipattern.description: Agile metrics (story points, velocity) presented directly to a steering committee that thinks in deliverables and labour cost.
antipattern.reason: Executive audiences interpret story points as arbitrary numbers; without translation they discount the data or demand a switch to hours, undermining agile capacity planning.

rule.statement: Set gate frequency proportional to risk: monthly for regulated programs, quarterly for steady-state, never weekly (that is just a status meeting renamed). Gate decisions — go/no-go — are sponsor calls only; agents may flag readiness but not declare it.
rule.rationale: Gate frequency mismatched to risk either throttles delivery (too frequent) or misses real exposure (too infrequent). Weekly gates produce decision fatigue and get rubber-stamped.

rule.statement: Re-baseline the predictive arm only when scope or contract changes, not when velocity slips. Velocity slippage is handled in retrospectives and planning; it is not a baseline trigger.
rule.rationale: Re-baselining on velocity drift obscures the original commitment and makes trend analysis impossible; the baseline is the contract, not the forecast.

antipattern.description: Applying Earned Value Management metrics (CPI, SPI computed from story-points x hourly rate) to the agile arm without an explicitly agreed conversion.
antipattern.reason: Story points are a relative planning unit, not a labour-cost proxy; EVM computed from them produces numbers that look precise but are meaningless, and finance will hold the PM accountable for them.

antipattern.description: Hybrid chosen as a political compromise rather than a risk-profile decision.
antipattern.reason: PMO-wants-control + teams-want-autonomy resolved by "we do hybrid" produces neither the governance of predictive nor the adaptability of agile — it produces incoherence dressed as methodology.

antipattern.description: Agile coach distrusts gantts; PM distrusts standups.
antipattern.reason: The dominant cultural failure in hybrid programs; the solution is cross-training: PMs shadow stand-ups, coaches attend gate reviews. Neither arm is optional.

see-also.link: Agile arm rituals for the delivery teams
Earned value baseline for the predictive arm
